title: Maternal resilience and anxiety as predictors of infant telomere length: A prospective study
author: Ciochoń, Aleksandra
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
ABSTRACT   
  Introduction: Maternal mental health during pregnancy plays an important role in fetal development and long-term health outcomes for the child. Chronic stress and prenatal anxiety may contribute to adverse health consequences, including telomere shortening, in offspring. Conversely, maternal resilience may buffer the negative effects of prenatal stress.  
  This study investigated the relationship between maternal trait anxiety and resilience, and infant’s telomere length (TL). We hypothesized that maternal resilience moderates the association between maternal anxiety and infant TL.   
  Methods: The study included 127 mothers (aged 21–41, M=31.51, SD=3.92) and their infants (at 3-11 months of age). Two standardized questionnaires were used to assess maternal trait anxiety (State-Trait Anxiety Inventory) and resilience (the Polish Scale for the Measurement of Resilience - SPP25). Telomere length was measured using quantitative PCR (qPCR) following a standard protocol. Mothers were categorized into high-resilience and low/moderate-resilience groups. A general linear model was used to examine the moderation effect, with dichotomized resilience as the moderator, infant TL as the dependent variable, and anxiety as the predictor, adjusting for maternal age, pre-pregnancy BMI, child sex and socioeconomic status.  
  Results: Although there was no significant direct association between maternal resilience and infant TL (r=0.004, p=0.96), and the relationship between maternal anxiety and infant TL was only marginally significant after adjusting for confounders (β=-0.18, p=0.056), a significant interaction effect between maternal anxiety and resilience on infant TL was observed (β=0.032, p=0.038). Simple effects analysis further revealed that the negative association between maternal anxiety and infant TL was significant in the high-resilience group (β=-0.41, p=0.006), but not in the low/moderate-resilience group (β=-0.014, p=0.908).  
  Conclusions: Our findings suggest that maternal anxiety and resilience interact to shape infant health, highlighting the important role of early-life factors in the context of long-term health outcomes.  
     
   Keywords:    telomere, infant health, pregnancy, mental health, resilience, anxiety, fetal development
